content.full_text:
State of California

Memorandum

To     : File -                                                    Date :July 18, 1983
                                                                   No. M-83-176




From : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
         Robert E. Leidigh

Subject: Applicability of Section 82033 (Interests in Real Property)
         to Third Parties - Discussed at 7/14/83 Advice Request
         Meeting - Arose from Telephone Request of George Shenas


         This topic was discussed at the 7/14/83 Advice Request
Meeting. The issue was whether Section 82033 applies to a third
party who is a source of income to an official. Specifically,
does the third party have an interest in real property held by
her spouse as his separate property. In the case of an official,
Section 82033 says "yes" the official has an interest in the
spouse's separate real property. In the case of the third party,
Section 82033 does not apply and the official may act to affect
her spouse's separate real property so long as there is no
"reasonably foreseeable" resulting "material" effect upon her.
In the case of separate property, we will assume that there is no
such effect unless facts are present to indicate to the contrary.

REL:plh

sections.question_synthetic: Whether Section 82033 (interests in real property) applies to third parties who are sources of income to officials, specifically whether a third party has an interest in real property held by an official's spouse as separate property.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Section 82033 applies to officials' interests in spouses' separate real property, but does not apply to third parties. An official may act to affect a spouse's separate real property so long as there is no reasonably foreseeable material effect on the third party; absent contrary facts, no such effect is presumed for separate property.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Internal FPPC memorandum rather than formal advice letter format; no formal QUESTION or CONCLUSION sections but contains clear legal analysis and determination; arose from telephone request by George Shenas discussed at 7/14/83 meeting; document formatted as memo to file.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether Section 82033 (interests in real property) applies to third parties who are sources of income to officials, specifically whether a third party has an interest in real property held by an official's spouse as separate property.

CONCLUSION: Section 82033 applies to officials' interests in spouses' separate real property, but does not apply to third parties. An official may act to affect a spouse's separate real property so long as there is no reasonably foreseeable material effect on the third party; absent contrary facts, no such effect is presumed for separate property.

Leidigh Subject: Applicability of Section 82033 (Interests in Real Property) to Third Parties - Discussed at 7/14/83 Advice Request Meeting - Arose from Telephone Request of George Shenas This topic was discussed at the 7/14/83 Advice Request Meeting. The issue was whether Section 82033 applies to a third party who is a source of income to an official. Specifically, does the third party have an interest in real property held by her spouse as his separate property. In the case of an official, Section 82033 says "yes" the official has an interest in the spouse's separate real property. In the case of the third party, Section 82033 does not apply and the official may act to affect her spouse's separate real property so long as there is no "reasonably foreseeable" resulting "material" effect upon her. In the case of separate property, we will assume that there is no such effect unless facts are present to indicate to the contrary. REL:plh
embedding.summary: FPPC memorandum addressing whether Section 82033 (interests in real property) applies to third-party income sources regarding an official's spouse's separate property. The FPPC concludes that Section 82033 does not apply to third parties, allowing officials to act on spouse's separate property absent reasonably foreseeable material effects.
